post: "Participating in this program changed the way I approach disagreement." That's Arianna Oropeza, a high school student from Miami, FL, one of the finalists going to London this November to debate her UK peers. BRI is proud to announce the 12 US finalists selected for Debating Matters: Transatlantic, a 5-day program in London this November where American high schoolers will join UK students to debate some of the most pressing issues of our time: artificial intelligence, digital surveillance, NATO, and free speech. These 12 were chosen from 50 students nationwide through a rigorous process of coaching sessions, practice debates, peer feedback, and a live qualifier. Civil discourse is a skill. These students are proof it can be learned. Thank you to Carnegie Corporation of New York for the generous investment that made this program possible. Meet the finalists:  https://bit.ly/4cXA3LY  #CarnegieCorporation  #BRI  #Debate  #CivilDiscourse

2 weeks left in thisCivic Star Challenge cycle. Here's what this has been about from the start: capturing what civic education looks like in America's 250th year. The Declaration isn't a relic. Liberty isn't abstract. And somewhere in your classroom, that's been demonstrated. That story belongs on the record. Share it before May 15. Enter the weekly $300 drawing. And help close out this historic school year the right way. Final entries here:  https://bit.ly/463KXxf  #CivicStarChallenge  #America250  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeacher  #BRI

The MyImpact Challenge closes in 5 days: May 3 at 11:59 PM PT. You already know whether you have students who qualify. You already know their project is real. The only question left is whether they submit it before the window closes, or let this be the year their work went unrecognized. A student in Austin, Texas built an accessible tennis program for children with disabilities. He almost didn't submit because he didn't think it was good enough. He won $10,000. Students from Fort Washington, Pennsylvania founded a youth-led policy think tank. Students in Hackensack, New Jersey built an environmental restoration nonprofit. Students in McLean, Virginia launched an initiative that's still growing. Every one of them submitted. The students who don't submit by May 3 will carry that question longer than they expect. If there's a teacher in your network who needs this reminder, send this to them. If there are students still on the fence, this is the moment. $40,000 in prizes. Open to students 13-19 across the country. 5 days. 👉  https://bit.ly/3fyGGr3  #MyImpactChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #FinalCall  #BRI

The people shaping civics in the Seattle region will be in the same room on April 30. We've brought together educators, civic leaders, and technology partners for a forum at MOHAI built around real conversation and collaborative problem-solving. If you work at the intersection of education, technology, or community then this is your room. Six days out. A few seats remain. April 30 | 6:30-9:30 PM | Museum of History and Industry, Seattle Register here: 🔗  https://bit.ly/3OOH0He  #CivicEducation  #Civics  #EdTech  #Seattle  #MOHAI  #CivicsInnovation  #BillOfRightsInstitute

Thomas Jefferson believed in natural rights, civic virtue, and self-government more eloquently than almost anyone in American history. He also knew slavery was wrong, and spent his life rationalizing why he couldn't act on that knowledge. What do we do with a founder who articulated the ideals of a free society but failed to live up to them? Historian Andrew Burstein argues we start by understanding before we judge. In this episode of BRI Scholar Talks, Burstein joins Tony Williams to examine the character behind the Declaration: the intellectual brilliance, the moral contradictions, and the enduring questions Jefferson's legacy leaves for every generation. Watch it here:  https://bit.ly/4tpRYBU  #AmericanHistory  #CivicEducation  #TeachingHistory  #SocialStudies  #BRI

Share this post. Forward the link. Let's make sure no great classroom story goes unrecognized. 13 days ago, 4 astronauts returned from the Moon. The question they left behind is one your students should be working through right now. The Artemis II mission made history on April 10, the first crewed lunar flyby in more than half a century. The mission was made possible by NASA's $24.4 billion federal budget and decades of sustained public investment in space exploration. Whether that investment should grow is a genuinely open question, and a civics-rich one. Think the Vote's latest debate: Should the U. S. government increase funding for space exploration and research? On one side: the argument that space investment drives technological innovation, strengthens national security in a competitive global environment, and inspires the next generation of STEM talent. GPS, satellite communications, and advances in medical imaging all have roots in space research. This is the case for exploration as forward-looking public investment. On the other: the argument that federal resources are finite, that domestic needs from infrastructure to healthcare remain pressing, and that private companies are increasingly capable of advancing space exploration without expanded government spending. This is a debate about national priorities, the proper scope of government, and how a republic decides where limited public resources go. Those are questions citizens, including the students in your classroom, have both the right and the responsibility to engage. Student submissions close May 7. Share it this week. 👉  https://bit.ly/4tu0UGE  #CivicsEducation  #ThinktheVote  #Artemis  #SpaceExploration  #NASA  #SocialStudies  #HighSchoolTeachers  #CriticalThinking  #BillofRightsInstitute  #STEMEducation

The MyImpact Challenge deadline is May 3. That's just 12 days. For teachers who've been meaning to submit with their students, or for students who've been working on a project but haven't started the application, here's exactly what a complete submission looks like. 3 components are required: 1. A written essay connecting the project to America's founding principles and civic virtues: what motivated the students, what values guided their work, and what they learned. 2. A project report documenting what was done, how, and what impact it had on the community. 3. Photo or video documentation: a visual record of the project in action. That's it. There's no trifold. No in-person presentation. No travel required. It's submitted entirely through the online portal at the link below, and it's open to students anywhere in the country: all 50 states, U. S. territories, and Armed Forces Schools Abroad. If you're working with students who have a real project underway, the essay is often the only piece that isn't already done. And with 12 days left, there's still time to finish it. 👉  https://bit.ly/3fyGGr3  #MyImpactChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #ServiceLearning  #BRI
